revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[Sue pottery]] and [[Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

[[Category:Completion tier 5]]
[[sn:Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun]]}}

{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24876586]] Wikidata name: Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

[[Category:Completion tier 5]]
[[sn:Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun]]}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24876586]] Wikidata name: Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.572306|N|130.701881|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ono Iwaya Kofun]][[en:Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun]][[ja:大野窟古墳]][[zh:大野窟古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24876586]] Wikidata name: Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.572306|N|130.701881|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]

The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].

{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.572306|N|130.701881|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]

==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].

Kofun]][[ja:大野窟古墳]][[zh:大野窟古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ono Iwaya Kofun]][[en:Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun]][[ja:大野窟古墳]][[zh:大野窟古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24876586]] Wikidata name: Ōno-no-Iwa Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | native_name =大野窟古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ono Iwaya Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun (The front part is on the left, and the rear mound is on the right.) | map_type = Japan Kumamoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.572306|N|130.701881|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa, Kumamoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Ryuhoku, Kumamoto| Ryūhoku]] neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Hikawa, Kumamoto|Hikawa]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]], opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]]. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] are unclear, but excavated items include [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Silla|Silla]]-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of [[:en:Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|Ogawa Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).

The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. <gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>

{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun}}, located in the {{ill|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|en|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|WD=Q7385497|lt= Ryūhoku}} neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Hikawa, Kumamoto|en|Hikawa, Kumamoto|WD=Q1184800|lt=Hikawa}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun}}, located in the {{ill|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|en|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|WD=Q7385497|lt= Ryūhoku}} neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Hikawa, Kumamoto|en|Hikawa, Kumamoto|WD=Q1184800|lt=Hikawa}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

{{ill|Hikawa, Kumamoto|en|Hikawa, Kumamoto|WD=Q1184800|lt=Hikawa}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus|1=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station|1=Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|1=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus|1=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station|1=Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|1=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Ono Iwaya Kofun}} *[https://www.town.hikawa.kumamoto.jp/kanko/kiji0033767/index.html HIkawa Town home page]

{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|34|20.3|N|130|42|6.77|E}}}}}} {{nihongo|'''Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun'''|大野窟古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the {{ill|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|en|Ryuhoku, Kumamoto|WD=Q7385497|lt= Ryūhoku|1=Ryuhoku, Kumamoto}} neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Hikawa, Kumamoto|en|Hikawa, Kumamoto|WD=Q1184800|lt=Hikawa|1=Hikawa, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2013.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/231048|title=大野窟古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest ''[[kofun]]'' in Kumamoto Prefecture, and is estimated to have been built in the mid-to-late 6th century (late Kofun period).
==Overview== The Ōno-no-Iwaya Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located on the Onohara Plateau in central Kumamoto Prefecture. The area is known as a site for many ancient Kofuns,including the [[Nozu Kofun Cluster]] located to the south, which has a separate National Historic Site designation. The existence of the tumulus has been known since the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and on the wall of its passageway is an inscription dated 1497 indicating that a statue of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was enshrined within; however, the tumulus was not [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] until 2003 to 2008.
The tomb is a "kenbishi"-type keyhole-shaped tomb with a protruding front end orientated to the south. The mound is built in two stages and has a total length of 122.8 meters, making it the largest in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is surrounded by a shield-shaped moat (excluding the front of the anterior part), measuring approximately 12 meters wide. The anterior part of the mound has an unusual style in which a row of earth pits (possibly related to rituals) replace the moat. The burial facility is a double-sided horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] with a house-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus|1=Sarcophagus}}, opening to the southwest. A 1.9-meter-wide stone shelf is installed on top of the sarcophagus, and two tuff mortuary beds are installed on each side near the entrance burial chamber is 6.5 meters high, making it the largest in Japan in terms of height. The sarcophagus is made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}}. No excavation has been conducted inside the burial chamber proper, so any {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} are unclear, but excavated items include {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}-style pottery from the moat pit], and a hat-shaped stone ornament from the mound proper. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
<gallery> Ono Iwaya Kofun, sekishitsu senmon.jpg|Entry to the burial chamber Ono Iwaya Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion now crowned with a Buddhist chapel </gallery>
The tumulus is about four kilometers southeast of {{ill|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|en|Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)|WD=Q5358104|lt=Ogawa Station|1=Ogawa Station (Kumamoto)}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|1=Kagoshima Main Line}}.
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|WD=Q6573273|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kumamoto)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
